coredata.dc:description: The urgent need for sustainable energy solutions and environmental monitoring necessitates innovative approaches to biomass utilization. This study explores unlocking the potential of rice husk pyrolysis oil for dual applications: producing high-energy solid fuel and environmentally sensitive carbon nanodots (B-CDs). Slow pyrolysis of rice husk oil, followed by atmospheric distillation, transforms the oil into bio-coal with a high calorific value of 29.85 MJ/kg, significant aliphatic and aromatic carbon content, lowest ash content, and abundant oxygen functional groups, making it a viable fossil fuel alternative. Further, oxidative crystalline cutting and surface modification of this bio-coal yield B-CDs that exhibit blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 41 %, an average size of 3.4 nm, and excellent photostability and hydrophilicity. Notably, these B-CDs are self-doped with nitrogen on their surface, enhancing their functionality. They demonstrate high sensitivity for Fe²⁺ detection, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.34 μM over a linear range of 0–100 μM, and a strong response to alkaline conditions. Validation experiments in tap and seawater samples confirmed the practical environmental applications of B-CDs. This study not only contributes to the advancement of pyrolysis oil as a source of fuel energy but also emphasizes the production of valuable carbon nanomaterials for environmental application.

head.ce:title: Unlocking the potential of rice husk pyrolysis oil: Dual production of high-energy solid fuel and Fe²⁺ detecting carbon nanodots for environmental application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Shipping and Carbon Neutrality Laboratory, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The urgent need for sustainable energy solutions and environmental monitoring necessitates innovative approaches to biomass utilization. This study explores unlocking the potential of rice husk pyrolysis oil for dual applications: producing high-energy solid fuel and environmentally sensitive carbon nanodots (B-CDs). Slow pyrolysis of rice husk oil, followed by atmospheric distillation, transforms the oil into bio-coal with a high calorific value of 29.85 MJ/kg, significant aliphatic and aromatic carbon content, lowest ash content, and abundant oxygen functional groups, making it a viable fossil fuel alternative. Further, oxidative crystalline cutting and surface modification of this bio-coal yield B-CDs that exhibit blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 41 %, an average size of 3.4 nm, and excellent photostability and hydrophilicity. Notably, these B-CDs are self-doped with nitrogen on their surface, enhancing their functionality. They demonstrate high sensitivity for Fe²⁺ detection, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.34 μM over a linear range of 0–100 μM, and a strong response to alkaline conditions. Validation experiments in tap and seawater samples confirmed the practical environmental applications of B-CDs. This study not only contributes to the advancement of pyrolysis oil as a source of fuel energy but also emphasizes the production of valuable carbon nanomaterials for environmental application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels are the primary energy source, but their production cycle takes a long time. It is essential to consider the environmental impact of our actions for sustainable development. Combustion of fossil fuels leads to air pollution and requires effective policies to reduce it and clean the environment. Coal is an abundant natural resource and an essential fossil fuel [1]. Relying heavily on coal and other natural fossil fuels for energy may require alternative sources in the future. Therefore, coal and other fossil fuels replacements with green environment technologies are becoming more widespread due to their safety for natural resources. Several research studies aim to replace fossil fuels with renewable and sustainable sources, mainly biomass [2].

Biomass is a sustainable and environmentally friendly energy source from living plants and agricultural waste. It can potentially reduce CO2, NOx, and SOx emissions and is used as raw material to produce biochar, and solid fuel as alternative energy sources [3–5]. Pyrolysis bio-oil from biomass pyrolysis is an alternative energy source. It has gained significant attention because it can effectively replace conventional fuels such as diesel, kerosene, coals, lignite, etc., [3–7].

Slow pyrolysis is typically used for producing biochar, while fast and flash pyrolysis is considered to produce bio-oil based on their heating rate, residence time, and product yield [8]. Bio-oil has some drawbacks, such as being highly thermally unstable, containing a high amount of oxygen, and having a low heating value. This is because bio-oil combines reactive organic compounds that contain oxygen, produced during cellulose/hemicellulose and lignin degradation during biomass pyrolysis [9–11]. Recently, bio-oil conversion techniques have been significantly focused on producing solid fuel. Solid fuel is produced from various pyrolysis bio-oil of agriculture wastes or biomass such as rice husk, sawdust, corn stem, and wheat straw, etc., by distillation process (atmosphere distillation and vacuum distillation) and HTC (Hydrothermal Carbonization) showed that bio coal produced from bio-oil was successfully replaced with natural resource, coal, and other fossil fuels [3,5,12]. Large-scale production of a powerful solid fuel can be quickly achieved from bio-oil, which is renewable. Production of solid fuel from bio-oil is very simple and can be produced by a distillation process [3,5,13]. This type of solid fuel is more stable than bio-oil, making it easier to store and transport for longer periods.

Previous research has reported high calorific values for bio-coal produced from bio-oil. Atmospheric distillation of fast pyrolysis bio-oil (rice husk) has achieved calorific values of 27.6 MJ/kg [3] while atmospheric vacuum distillation of bio-oil from wheat straw has yielded a range of 27.18–29.05 MJ/kg [5]. These findings highlight the promising potential of bio-oil as a solid fuel alternative.

In recent years, research on the application of synthesized carbon dots from natural fossil fuels, such as raw coals (including bituminous coal, anthracite, and lignite), coal derivatives (same as coke and coal tar) [14], petrochemical coke, heavy oil [15], because of high carbon containment and their carbon structure. Carbon dots are tiny carbon base nanoparticles with a size between 10 nm and zero dimension, which were discovered accidentally in 2004 [16]. CDs have highly advantageous physical characteristics, high water solubility, strong photostability, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity [16,17].

Carbon dots (CDs) are divided into carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, and carbon nanodots from numerous types of carbon sources with their different application in bioimaging, sensor assays, cell imaging, solar cells, photocatalysts [16], removal of the oil spill [18], supercapacitor[19] because of their unique properties, high quantum yields, surface functionalization, and low cost[20]. Heavy metal detection in water using carbon dots is one of the popular methods due to its cost-effectiveness, simple preparation, and environmentally friendly nature. Excessive amounts of Fe ions (iron) in water can pose health risks to humans. Therefore, detecting Fe ions is crucial for environmental remediation and health monitoring in various sources such as tap water, lake water, and human blood [21–23].

Synthesis of carbon dots from different kinds of biomass wastes and other natural resources that contain high carbon content, including coal, biochar, and coke, has been studied by using various methods such as microwave-assisted, hydrothermal methods, solvothermal methods, ultra-sonicated methods, electrochemical etching, carbonization, and refluxing methods [20,24,25]. In 2013, Ye and his research group developed graphene quantum dots from different fossil fuels and coals based on their carbon structure [26]. This suggests that precursors containing graphene, graphite, and other carbon structures can produce carbon dots. Carbon dots produced from coal and biomass are widely attractive due to their cost-effectiveness, but saving natural resources and large-scale production remains challenging [27].

Despite its potential, slow pyrolysis oil faces several limitations, including lower yield, heating values, aging, poor stability, quality, and complex composition. Previous research has primarily focused on improving bio-oil quality through various technologies. However, it is crucial to explore new applications for slow pyrolysis oil and develop advanced green technologies for solid fuels beyond conventional burning. Our study introduces a novel method for producing solid fuel from slow pyrolysis oil via atmospheric distillation, which can then be used as a carbon precursor to synthesize blue fluorescent carbon dots (BCDs). This approach allows for the large-scale production of nanocarbon dots with increased photostability and higher quantum yield than those produced from fossil fuels, such as coal. This study comprehensively analyzed morphology, thermal stability, structure, functional bonding, chemical composition, solid fuel properties, and carbon dots' properties. The resulting carbon dots were utilized for detecting heavy metals in water, with Fe2+ ions demonstrating high sensitivity and efficient quenching in real water samples. This study offers insight into the potential applications of slow pyrolysis oil beyond its traditional use in chemical and energy production, showcasing its potential in sustainable solid fuel and the production of carbon nanomaterials.

Rice husk, FeCl3 (≥99.99 %), CuSO4·5 H2O (99.99 %), CaCl2·2 H2O (99.99 %,), CoCl2 (99.7 %) ZnCl2 (99.999 %,), CdCl2 (99.99 %,), MgSO4 (99.99 %,), NaCl (99.99 %,), MnCl2·4 H2O (99.99 %,), FeCl2·4 H2O (99.95 %,), NiCl2·6 H2O (99.9 %,), PbCl2 (99.99 %,), K2CO3 (99.99 %,), AlCl3 (99.99 %,), CrCl3 (99.99 %,), Quinine sulfate, Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2-30 %, Shanghai), 0.22 μm polytetrafluorethylene membrane were purchase and used.

The process of preparing bio-coal is shown in Fig. 1. In this study, our represented sample is rice husks, which will mainly analyze bio-coal properties and synthesis of carbon dots. The initial step involves slow pyrolysis of renewable biomass such as rice husk, rice straw, and corn cob without oxygen at 550°C to produce bio-oil and biochar.

Initially, 400 g of each biomass was obtained from the Dalian Maritime University campus in Liaoning, China. The samples were dried at 100°C for 12 hours, and then the bio-oil was produced using a steel batch reactor designed and built at the laboratory. To remove air from the reactor, the biomass was placed in the pyrolysis reactor with a feed pipe under N2 flow (430 ml/min) for 30 minutes. The biomass (rice husk) was heated at a constant temperature of around 550°C for 1 hour, producing 90 ml of bio-oil and 360 g of biochar. Bio-coal was produced by atmospheric distillation of rice husk bio-oil at room temperature. For this, a round-bottom flask was used, and 90 ml of bio-oil was slowly heated at around 250°C with magnetic stirring. The bio-oil was distilled and condensed at elevated temperatures until bio-oil residue was left. The bath was kept at room temperature to cool down, and finally, atmospheric bio-oil distillation residue was turned into black solid fuel (Bio-coal). The Bio-Coal was ground, sieved (120 mesh), dried in an oven at around 70°C to remove water, and kept in a desiccator for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (BCDs) from bio-coal
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare Bio-Coal base Carbon dots (BCQDs), 0.5 g of expanded Bio-Coal was mixed with 2 ml of 30 % hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 10 minutes to promote particle promotion through high-frequency sound waves. Next, 300 ml of deionized water was added to the mixture and stirred for 12 hours to form a homogeneous solution. The final mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 minutes and then passed through a 0.22 μm sterile filter to obtain BCQDs. It's important to note that BCDs were prepared without dialysis.

The fluorescence quantum yield of BCDs was determined by using relative methods. Quinie Sulpte was selected as a reference sample (QY=54 %, 01MSO4). The QY of BC-CDs was calculated by Eq. (1)

All BCDs and reference samples were prepared until absorbance intensity was < 0.05 at λex=350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heavy metal ion quenching assay and limit of detection (LOD)
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the application of BCDs as fluorescence-based sensors to heavy metal ions detection from water experiments performed with Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, K2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ under comparable experimental conductions. Every 2 ml of BC-CDs solution was added to each heavy metal ion solution with DI water (10uM). For future study of the sensitive detections of Fe2+ a series solution of FeCl2·4 H2O with concentrations of 0–100uM were prepared and mixed with 2 ml of BCDs. All measurements were taken at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and emission was recorded between 370 nm and 600 nm. The fluorescence reduction percentage (%) of heavy metals was determined as the difference between the initial and final fluorescence and calculated using the formula [17] below (Eq. 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample assay for testing metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
We collected tap water samples from our laboratory and seawater samples from the surface of Xinghai Bay Beach in Dalian, China, at latitude 38.876701 and longitude 121.565674. The water samples were then filtered using syringe filters with 0.22μm pore size, spiked with varying concentrations of Fe2+, and mixed with BCDs. The samples were incubated at 37°C for 25 minutes before their fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. To investigate the FL quenching of Fe 2+ in different concentrations with BCDs in water, real sample water (Tap and Sea water), photostability test, and pH test were determined by Stern-Volmer Equation- (Equation −4)

where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of BCDs with present and absent metal ions in the sample. Q is the concentration of Fe2+, and Ksv is the quenching constant of Stern-Volmer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization techniques of bio coals and BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: An elemental analyzer (German–elemental Unicube) was used to determine the content of C, H, and N in the bio-coal and bio-oil samples. The thermostability and combustion of the bio-coal were analyzed using a Thermogravimetric (TA-TGA 550-USA). The sample was heated at a rate of 20 ◦C/min, from 80 ◦C to 800 ◦C, and held at that temperature for 1 minute. The flow rate of O2 was maintained at 40 ml/min throughout the TGA experiment. The HHVs were determined by a calorimeter (SJLRY-8000, China). The ignition temperature (Ti) is the point at which a substance rapidly transitions from a stable state to a highly oxidizing state. This value is determined using the TG-DTG tangent method. The burnout temperature (Tb) is defined as the temperature at which 98 % of the substance's total weight loss occurs during combustion. The peak burning rate (Rm) is identified at the highest point on the DTG curves, while the average burning rate is referred to as Ra. The combustibility index of bio-coal is calculated using the formula C = Rm/Ti². The complete combustion characteristics index (CCI) of bio-coal is calculated using the formula CCI = (Rm * Ra) / (Ti² * Tb).

Moreover, the proximate analysis (FC and VM) was measured using a proximate analyzer, and all measurements were conducted according to China's national standards (GB/T 212 2008 and GB/T 213 2008). To determine the fuel ratio, used the formula FC/VM.

The bio-oil composition was analyzed using GC-MS (GC- 7890A- Agilent, USA) with a DB-5 ms capillary column (30 m x 250 mm×0.25 mm). The injection port was maintained at 250 °C, and 1 μL sample was injected. Helium (≥99.99 % pure) (1.00 ml/min) was used as the carrier gas. The column oven was initially set at 40 °C held for 1 min, then programmed to 100 °C at 10 °C·min−1 maintained for 2 min, and finally, the temperature increased to 280 °C at 20 °C·min−1 min, where it was held for 1 min. The source and transfer line temperature were maintained at 280 °C. The splitting ratio was 1:20. The solvent delay time is 1.5 min. The organic substances identified were matched with the NIST spectrum library and literature sources.

The yields of bio coals were calculated based on the mass of different bio-oils obtained from pyrolysis and the mass of bio coals received after distillation and cooled under room temperature. Morphology analysis was conducted using Scanning electron microscopy SEM (Japan Hitachi Regulus 8100).

Raman analysis of bio coal and carbon dots by using Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution, Japan. XPS was conducted on Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi (USA). The optical absorption spectrum of BCDs was obtained using a JINGHUA-754 PC/UV–VIS spectrophotometer. The fluorescence intensities were determined by the F97-Pro photo-spectrometer. The decay lifetime of carbon dots was tested with a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK). The morphology, size distribution, and shape of carbon dots were analyzed using TEM and HRTEM with instrument model JEM2100HR at 200 kV. The chemical bonding of bio coal and surface functional groups of carbon dots were determined using KBr pellets on a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Bruker, Alpha-T) with a wavelength range of 400 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1.

The composition of bio-oil and distillate bio-oil from rice husk is detailed in Tables S (1) and S(2). The analysis reveals that rice husk bio-oil comprises a diverse range of organic compounds, including acids, esters, alkanes, aldehydes, furans, amides, phenols, ketones, and various unidentified components, both before and after distillation. This indicates that bio-oil is a complex mixture with a high oxygen content. The bio-oil obtained from slow pyrolysis contains a significant amount of water, mixed with light and heavy oil fractions. Additionally, bio-oils derived from various biomass sources consistently show approximately 35 % oil phase and 65 % aqueous phase.

During bio-oil distillation, bio coal can be produced as a hydrocarbon source for carbon dots precursors, similar to coal [28]. The analysis of bio-coal indicated that it contains higher amounts of carbon and hydrogen while having lower levels of oxygen than distilled bio-oil (as shown in Table 1). This suggests that some light oxygen-containing groups in the bio-oil were removed during the distillation process. The high carbon content of bio coal makes it suitable for producing carbon dots as a raw carbon source. The carbon content of rice husk bio-coal increased significantly from 39.97 % to 71.08 % after bio-oil distillation.

The hydrogen (H) content of bio coal also rose from 5.14 % to 6.25 %, compared to the H content of bio-oil. However, bio-coal’s oxygen (O) content decreased from 53.34 % to 18.74 %, compared to bio-oil oxygen content. Similar to conventional coal, bio coal also contains a high carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen content and small amounts of sulfur and nitrogen that are beneficial in synthesizing self-doped S and N carbon quantum dots [28].

The quality of solid fuel can be evaluated using the Higher Heating Value (HHV), which is the most critical parameter for this assessment. The HHV of bio-oils derived from cornstalk, rice straw, and rice husk are approximately 12.56 MJ/kg, 13.68 MJ/kg, and 15.68 MJ/kg, respectively. These values are notably lower than the HHV of bio-coal produced from the same feedstocks, as shown in Table 1.

The HHV values for bio-coals produced via slow pyrolysis from rice husk, rice straw, and cornstalk are 29.85 MJ/kg, 28.34 MJ/kg, and 27.42 MJ/kg, respectively. These values are higher than those of lignite coal and are comparable to other high-rank coals such as sub-bituminous, bituminous, and anthracite coals. Notably, the ash content of all bio-coals is significantly lower than that of commercial coals, despite having a higher volatile matter content (VM).

Among the different types of bio-coal, the yield from rice husk is higher than that from other biomass-based bio-coals. According to literature, solid fuels produced from slow pyrolysis exhibit higher HHV values than bio-oil derived from fast pyrolysis and vacuum distillation. These comparisons with commercial coals are summarized in Table 2. [3,5,13].

Van Krevelen plots were used to determine the degree of coalification of bio-coal obtained through slow pyrolysis at atmospheric distillation, as shown in Fig. 2. These plots compare the H/C and O/C atomic ratios of various types of bio-coal, including Rice Husk, Rice Straw, and Corn Cob, with our standard sample coal.

All of the bio-coals produced by slow pyrolysis exist in our stand anthracite coal region of the Van Krevelen plot because their H/C atomic ratios were reduced, and the O/C atomic ratios of bio-coals are somewhere between that of sub-bituminous and lignite coals. The degree of coalification divides coalification into different coal ranks or stages. The decreasing value of the H/C and O/C atomic ratios, hydrogen content, and oxygen content is related to the increasing calorific value of bio-coals. Furthermore, Bio coal contains low ash content. Due to its high carbon content, calorific value, and degree of coalification, eco-friendly bio-coal production is a viable substitute for commercial solid fuel and carbon-based nanomaterial.

The morphology of bio-coal (rice husk) was provided by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and its images showed that bio-coal is amorphous and less porous, as shown in Fig. 3. Moreover, the bio-coal generated from bio-oil distillation contains black solid glossy; some have smooth surfaces and a blank tink similar to commercial coal. They have rough, smooth, and simple structures of cloudy shapes, and it looks like carbon contains organic particles that are compactly formed.

The FTIR spectra of both bio-oil and bio-coal reveal the presence of various oxygenated functional groups. Key peaks include the O-H stretch at 3405 cm⁻¹, indicative of alcohol groups, and the CO stretch observed between 1704 and 1730 cm⁻¹, associated with carbonyl groups in lignin and hemicellulose. Additionally, phenolic hydroxyl (C-O at 1212 cm⁻¹) and ether (C-O-C at 1084 cm⁻¹) groups were detected, highlighting the complex nature of these bio-derived materials. These functional groups are essential for the chemical reactivity and environmental sensing applications of the derived carbon dots [31–34].

The analysis showed that bio-coal is rich in oxygen-containing groups such as C-O-C, C-O (including O-H alcohol and phenol), and CO groups. These groups are vital for enhancing the surface properties of bio-coal, particularly in synthesizing carbon dots that are useful for environmental sensing applications, especially in detecting metal ions [31–39].

The presence of these oxygenated functional groups increases the reactivity of bio-coal during combustion, resulting in more efficient and complete combustion. This enhanced reactivity improves energy output and positions bio-coal as a promising alternative solid fuel [40,41].

Additionally, the hydroxyl (OH) and carbonyl (CO) groups contribute to improved ignition properties, allowing bio-coal to ignite at lower temperatures. This characteristic reduces the energy required for initial combustion, making bio-coal a more accessible and environmentally friendly fuel option, thus supporting sustainable energy practices [41].

The FTIR analysis also confirmed the presence of aromatic carbon rings, characterized by sp² carbon hybridization, with prominent peaks observed at 1456 cm⁻¹, 1516 cm⁻¹, and 1604 cm⁻¹. Additionally, aliphatic hydrocarbons, indicative of sp³ carbon hybridization, were identified by their characteristic absorption bands at 2962 cm⁻¹, 2926 cm⁻¹, and 1375 cm⁻¹. These findings suggest that bio-coal contains both aromatic and aliphatic structures, which are crucial for the stability and structural integrity of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from this material [42,43]. The FTIR analysis results indicate that bio-coal is suitable for synthesizing carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots, containing both sp² and sp³ carbon hybridizations. Additionally, there is no significant change in the functional groups of both bio-coal and bio-oil obtained from slow pyrolysis which is shown in Fig. 4(a).

The TG and DTG curves of bio-coal and commercial coals, illustrated in Fig. 4(c-d), provide a comprehensive comparison of their combustion performance. According to the TGA results, significant weight loss of about 90 % occurs between 250°C and 600°C for both bio-coal and commercial coals. This substantial weight loss indicates the primary combustion phase where most of the volatile matter and fixed carbon are burned off. Complete combustion of the organic content in bio-coal, as well as in commercial Coals A and B, results in a weight loss of 98.33 % at temperatures above 650°C. This indicates that nearly all the combustible material has been oxidized by this point.

The residual ash content is approximately 7–9 % for both bio-coal and commercial coals. This similarity in ash content demonstrates that bio-coal has combustion characteristics comparable to those of commercial coals, suggesting its viability as an alternative fuel source. The complete combustion in an O2 atmosphere further validates the efficiency and potential of bio-coal in mimicking the combustion behavior of traditional commercial coals.

Based on the DTG curve of bio coal, it can be observed that there were three peaks. The first two small peaks appeared at 250–400◦C, which might have been due to the primary combustion of the macromolecule [44]. During this stage, volatile substances may be released from the decomposition of macromolecules, leading to char formation. The other peak was observed at 450–650°C and was related to fixed carbon combustion [5].

Table 3 presents a detailed analysis of the Thermogravimetric (TG) and Derivative Thermogravimetric (DTG) curves to compare the combustion characteristics of bio-coal with commercial coals. Bio-coal demonstrated a lower initial decomposition temperature (Ti) at 263.34°C compared to Coal A at 315.03°C and Coal B at 279.39°C, indicating it ignites more easily. Despite its lower thermal stability, bio-coal exhibits a relatively high peak decomposition rate (Rm) of −12.55 %/min, which is intermediate between Coal A at −18.9 %/min and Coal B at −9.96 %/min. This suggests that bio-coal has a balanced combustion process.

The burnout temperature (Tb) of Bio-coal is 600.21°C, which is lower than both Coal A and Coal B. This signifies a shorter combustion duration, making it advantageous for applications requiring quick energy release. Additionally, the Combustion Reactivity (C) of Bio-coal is 1.81, which is close to the Coal A value, indicating efficient combustion performance. The Combustion Characteristic Index (CCI) of Bio-coal is 1.92, demonstrating strong combustion capabilities, closely following the Coal A value of 2.67 and significantly higher than the Coal B value of 1.31.

These findings suggest that the combustion characteristics of Bio-coal are superior to those of lignite (Coal B) and closely resemble those of higher-rank anthracite (Coal A). The thermogravimetric analysis results indicate that bio coal (rice husk) exhibits superior combustion properties, including a higher combustibility index and complete combustion characteristics [45]. Notably, the Combustion Reactivity (C) and Combustion Characteristic Index (CCI) of Bio-coal produced from slow pyrolysis oil are higher compared to bio-coal produced from fast pyrolysis oil, indicating improved performance

Raman analysis is a good technique for analyzing carbon-based materials with a degree of graphitization. Generally, the Raman peak at around 1600 cm−1 is related to perfect-order carbon called the G band. However, a peak present, between 1360 and 1400 cm−1, called the D band, shows a defect or disorder of amorphous carbon [46].

For Bio coal, there are two peaks observed at around 1370 cm −1 for the D band, which is mainly related to aromatic rings, aromatic (≥ 6 fused benzene ring) sp3 hybridized carbon, and 1573 cm−1 for the G band because of graphite
E
2 g
2
, related to Sp2 carbon frameworks showed Fig. 4-b. No extra overtone of the D band, 2D, or another band in the band position occurred, similar to bituminous commercial coal [47]. The intensities ratio of bio-coal (ID/IG) is 0.72, which indicates that bio-coal has a relatively high proportion of disordered or amorphous carbon structure compared to graphitic structure because bio-coal is a complex organic mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and structural characteristic of BCDs

ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we mainly focused on and synthesized carbon dots from rice husk bio coal. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis revealed that the Bio Coal-derived carbon dots (BCDs) has a quasi-spherical shape with an average size of 3.4 nm (shown Fig. 5b-c). The combination of the quasi-spherical shape and surface states contributes to the excitation-dependent emission property of BCDs. The compact, quasi-spherical structure of BCDs contributes to their excellent photostability and resistance to photobleaching. This stability is crucial for long-term performance in photoluminescence applications.

Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) showed that the lattice fringe spacing or distance of 0.34 nm (showed Fig. 5 -c), which belongs to the (002) plane of graphite structure, confirmed the well-crystallized graphite nature of the carbon dots [48].

This indicates the presence of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a lattice structure within the BCDs, forming a graphitic core that provides stability to the overall structure [42]. The graphitic domains in BCDs are typically very small, often just a few nanometers in size, leading to quantum confinement effects. This confinement of electrons within the graphitic structure contributes to the fluorescence properties of BCDs. The combination of this structure, quantum confinement effects, and surface states results in the unique optical properties of BCDs, making them promising materials for various applications in bioimaging, sensing, and optoelectronics [42,48].
The SAED patterns of representative BCDS are displayed in Fig. 5 d. The FFT image and SAED pattern image BCDs exhibit appropriate graphitic crystalline structures [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation from XPS, FTIR, and Raman analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The element composition and functionalities of BCDs were confirmed via XPS analysis. The XPS results shown in Fig. 6 demonstrated that BCDs mainly contain different binding energy peaks at round in 285 eV (C1s), 532.5 eV (O1s), and 399.8 eV (N1s), which include different atomic of carbon 81.2 %, oxygen 15 %, and nitrogen 3.7 % respectively. This suggested that BCDs has a high amount content of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen group on the surface of carbon dots. The high resolution of XPS analysis was also provided for future study, as shown in Fig. 6-b, c, d.

Fig. 6-a showed that the high-resolution XPS of the C1s band was deconvoluted into four peaks, 284.1 eV, 284.7 eV, 285.5 eV, and 286.5 eV attributed to CC/C-C (related to Sp2), C-H (associated with Sp3), C-N and CO /C-OH respectively. The high resolution of O1s mainly contains two peaks, 531.3 eV and 532.7 eV, attributed to CO and C-OH/C-O-C [28]There are two peaks,399.8 eV and 401.1 eV, which can be recognized as the presence of nitrogen-containing group amine and graphitic N group, as shown in Fig. 6-d.

The high-resolution XPS binding energy confirmed that BCDs have sp2 and sp3 carbon and a high amount of oxygen exists on their surface [49]. Furthermore, XPS analysis confirmed the self-dropping of nitrogen atoms on the surface of carbon dots, and FTIR analysis confirmed it. The incorporation of these groups into BCDs results in an improvement in their overall stability and ability to dissolve in water. Therefore, carbon quantum dots synthesized via bio coal are supported by a self-dropping heteroatom, mainly nitrogen, which can encourage high sensing and promising for use in various applications. The surface functionalization of carbon dots showed that there are O-H/N-H, CO, CC, and H-C vibration modes, as shown in Fig. 7- b. The broad absorption peak of BCDs at around 3040 cm−1 to 3745 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of O-H and N-H. This indicates the presence of hydroxyl groups and amine groups, sp3 hybridizing on the surface of carbon dots.

Additionally, other sp2 hybridizing peaks observed at 1600 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 correspond to CO (assumed that they were obtained from lignin and hemicellulose of bio coal) and CC vibrations, respectively. The characteristics of the carbon dots surface functional group demonstrate that it mainly contains and is rich in hydrophilic functional groups [50]. The Stretching Vibration of the CN peak occurred at around 2060–2260 cm−1, which was not observed in the FTIR spectrum of raw bio-coal [51,52]. This indicated that a chemical transformation of carbon dots occurred during the treatment process with bio coal. This is possible due to the self-doping of nitrogen and nitrile functional groups formed on the surface of carbon dots. The changes in this process were observed in both FTIR spectra of Fe2+ ions with BCDs before and after the situation, confirming the occurrence of chemical transformation.

The self-doping of heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N) on the surface of carbon dots confirmed that carbon dots delivered from bio coal have similar characteristics to those produced from some conventional coal and other biomass [53]. Therefore, FTIR confirmed significant changes in the surface functional groups after treatment. It can be assumed that Fe2+ bonds with nitrogen atoms on the surface of carbon dots, which can be intense and occur in a functional group, becoming a quenching process.

Raman spectra of carbon dots also showed two band positions at around 1369 cm−1 for the D band and 1602 cm−1 for the G band. These peaks are related to the D band, which is responsible for the defected surface, and the G band for the Sp2 carbon framework from BCDs. D-bands in BCDs can be attributed to surface oxidation and small size [47].

In Raman analysis, the degree of disorder or defects in bio coal and carbon dots using parameters like intensities and area ratios. The integrated intensity ratios of the D band (amorphous) and G band (crystalline) of bio-coal (ID/IG) is 0.72, which is increase to the (ID/IG) ratio of carbon dots to 0.81 after H2O2 oxidative and exfoliation due to the structural modification introduced on the surface of carbon dots or introduced of defects to the basal planes and the edges of carbon dots [26].

The D-band (ID) intensities for all bio-coal and carbon dots are lower than those of their G-band (IG), which suggests that they all have an excellent graphitization degree of synthesizing. Moreover, the increasing (ID/IG) ratio of carbon dots to 0.81 showed close to the graphite-like structure [25]. The peak area of the D-band and G-band was applied to determine the concentration of defect in graphitic structure or quantitatively analyze between small and large aromatic rings. Bio coal has a higher ratio (AD/AG) of 1.87 than carbon dots 0.63, which means that the concentration of aromatic rings changed after synthesizing carbon dots from bio coal.

The amount of the medium or larger aromatic ring structures in bio coal is the deceased in the carbon dots due to the transformation of aromatic changes during the separation, purification, oxidation, and other effects like ultrasonication of the synthesis process of carbon dots from bio coal [54,55]. This phenomenon indicates that the distillation of bio-oil residue produces an amorphous organic carbon mixture called bio-coal, which can synthesize carbon dots with a graphite-like structure, BCDs.

The optical properties of as-prepared BCDs were investigated through UV–vis absorption, FL excitation, and emission spectra. The photostability and effect of different pH values on BCDs were also examined. In UV-Vis, there are different absorption peaks for BCDs, BCDs with Fe2+, and absorption peaks for Fe2+, as shown in Fig. 8-c. UV-Vis absorption spectra of carbon dots typically showed two broad absorption peaks, between 220 and 280 nm, which are attributed to the π-π* transition of the carbon core involving aromatic sp2 carbons (aromatic CC bonds), and the second peak occurs between 280 and 400 nm which is related to carbon functional groups containing oxygen, n-π* transition of CO/C-O bond generated by the surface band [15,56].

It is important to note a significant change in the carbon core region of BCDs during the UV-Vis analysis. This change was observed in the new absorbance at 306 nm and 310 nm after Fe2+ was mixed with Carbon dots. The change resulted from coordination or chelation and complex interactions between carbon dots and heavy metal ions.

Additionally, BCDs emit blue light due to the recombination of excited electrons from the n-π* transition of CO. The FL emission spectrums of BCDs, performed at different excitation wavelengths, are presented in Fig. 8-a and b. The FL specters of BCDs indicated their unique properties and showed an excitation-dependent emission wavelength characteristic.

The Fig. 8-a displays the highest intensities of emission wavelength (370–600 nm) received from 360 nm excitation wavelength. BCDs’ photoluminescence (PL) spectrum had a high quantum yield (QY=41 %) and shifted to the blue region with increased excitation wavelength. This shift occurred due to numerous chromophore or fluorophore systems in the aromatic and oxidation groups, which FTIR and XPS analysis confirmed. Thus, the fluorescence properties of carbon dots from bio coal are nearly similar to those produced from sub-bituminous coal [57]. Furthermore, the PL of carbon dots from bio-coal has shown an FWHM below 100 nm, confirming that CDs from bio-coal have significant heterogeneous size distributions [58].

Firstly, the thermal stability of BCDs was tested at different temperatures from 30 °C to 90 °C for 1 hour each. No significant change exists under different thermal ranges, as higher temperatures cannot change and modify any functional chemical group structure of BCDs. Thus, BCDs showed high thermal stability and minimum recombination nonradiative process or defect from the excited state [59].

Furthermore, even after three months kept under room temperature, the FL intensities of BCDs did not significantly change long-time storage, which suggested that BCDs has excellent photostability. The high thermal and Fl stability is related to the strong protection of oxygen-rich functional groups. These groups can help control the aggregation and graphitic shell, protecting the carbon core from external factors that could lead to oxidation or degradation. This was confirmed with graphitic area AD/AG of BCDs in Raman analysis and FTIR. Thus, the fluorescence and photostability of BCDs is independent of external effect, heat, and storage time because of their rich surface functional group and well-graphitic structure.

Based on the FTIR and XPS analyses, there is a rich of oxygenated carboxylic (-COOH) and self-dropped amine (-NH2) functionalities on its surface, providing a high oxidation and reduction potential of BCDs. Thus, the pH may be highly affected by the sensing application. It was monitored under different pH value tests (pH 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13) to explore the sensing application of BCDs under the pH effect. As shown in Fig. 9-a, under pH-1, strong acid conditions, and the highest alkaline state, pH-13 showed the weakest fluorescence emissions. This phenomenon occurred due to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface of BCDs (-OH/-COOH, -NH2). Among them, pH-13 showed the lowest fluorescence emission, which indicates that under a strong alkali state, it has intense deprotonation.[58]. Under acidic conditions, -COOH mainly occupies the surface of BCDs, and the electron-hole pair recombination process is obstructed, which can lead to fluorescence emission reduction. On the other hand, amine (-NH2) is a high electron-donating functional group on the surface of BCDs under strong alkali conditions [59–61].The highest fluorescence emission showed under the pH-7 state, so we adjusted to a neutral pure pH value of 7 with different metals to perform the heavy metals sensing test.

Furthermore, BCDs can be used as pH sensors, especially for strong alkali conditions. The pH sensing test confirmed that carbon dots produced from bio coal are highly suitable due to the introduction of rich Sp2 and Sp3 carbon on the surface of carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and sensitivity of BCDs for Fe2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the heavy metals sensing ability of BCDs, BCDs solution was added to different metal ions containing water solutions, and detection experiments were performed with Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, K2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ under the same conditions.

All measurements were taken at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and emission was recorded between 320 and 650 nm. Fig. 8-d showed that Fe2+ containing solution showed higher efficiency fluorescence quenching or reducing than other metal ions. The metal ions with BCDs absorb the energy in the excited state and relax it to the ground state without the photon, which can cause no fluorescence emission or luminescence quenching process. The FL spectra of BCDs with different Fe2+ concentrations (0–100 μM) were recorded in Fig. 10 a-b.

The result showed that Fe2+concentrations have a good linear correlation F0/F = 0.0062 [Fe2+] + 0.9551, R² = 0.9902. The LOD of Fe2+ is 0.33 μM. The concentration of Fe2+ increased, and the fluorescence emission decreased, which means that Fe2+ is a highly sensitive quencher of luminescence with BCDs, indicating that the sensor is susceptible to detecting Fe2+. Thus, carbon dots synthesis from alternative solid fuel (bio-coal) was successfully applied to detecting Fe2+ with high QY%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible formation mechanism of bio coal and bio coal-derived carbon quantum dots (BCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: When bio-oil is distilled at 250°C, the oxygenated organic compounds vaporize, removing the volatile components. The complex compounds that are left after high boiling point components don't vaporize and form bio-coal as a black residue. These compounds undergo polymerization and condensation between phenols, aldehydes, and pyrolytic lignin oligomers. This process is similar to producing phenolic resins, where phenols react with aldehydes and are catalyzed by an acidic or base [62].

As the process of polymerization proceeds, additional monomers are added, creating high molecular weight compounds with extended aromatic systems and a significant degree of cross-linking. These more massive, condensed aromatic structures become insoluble and separate from the solution as a black solid residue. The end product is a carbon-rich black solid material, so-called bio-coal, which can be used as fuel. Fig. 11

The resulting polymerized organic carbon chain is then subjected to oxidizing cutting and surface modification using hydrogen peroxide H2O2. The *OH radical from H2O2 is performed as a powerful oxidizing species that can lead to the oxidization of the organic carbon chain into CO2 and H2O under lower temperatures [63]. During the oxidizing cutting process, more significant graphitized and non-graphitized polyaromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are broken down into smaller fragments, forming stable crystalline carbon dots with a graphitic core [63]. The TEM analysis confirmed the presence of this graphitic core. Table 4

It was additionally confirmed that the surface modification of carbon dots was altered significantly, resulting in an oxidized or reduced quantity of aliphatic groups (-CH2, -CH3) and phenol/alcohol groups (C-O-C/C-O) in bio coal due to the breakdown by *OH in the reaction, which leads to edge carbon and introduce oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups [63–65]. This was further confirmed by FTIR analysis. In the process of the transformation of carbon nanodots with H2O2 oxidizing exfoliation from bio-coal, hydroxyl radical plays a key role.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of quenching Fe 2+ with BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching is typically categorized into internal filtering (IFE) and ion-binding effects [66]. The ion-binding effect includes dynamic and static quenching mechanisms [67]. To explore the mechanism of the quenching process with BCDs and Fe2+, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and FTIR analysis before and after the reaction of BCDs with Fe2+ and the different FL lifetimes decay were analyzed. It was found that there is a slight change and decreased absorption peaks found at 306 nm and 310 nm in the region of n-π* transition of the CO/C-O bond generated by the surface band, which was consistent with the properties of static quenching (showed in Fig. 8-a).

We confirmed this change by studying the FTIR spectra of the BCDs solution before and after adding Fe2+, as shown in Fig. 7-b. In FTIR, the vibration strength of O-H and /N-H, CO, and CC bonds showed strong stretching vibration after adding BCDs with Fe2+. Furthermore, FTIR confirmed that the BCDs was activated with Fe2+, which obtained new strong peaks for the Fe-O bond (stretching vibration from 499 cm−1 to 689 cm−1) [22]. These observations suggest that the heavy metal ion Fe2+ is the highly sensitive coordinate bond formation between the Fe2+ and O atoms on the surface of BCDs [23] and react with various functional groups, such as -OH, -NH2, CO, CC, and C-H, present on the surface of BCDs.

Interestingly, the nitrogen-containing functional group CN exhibited high sensitivity in FTIR analysis (Fig. 7b) after adding Fe2+ metal ions. This proves that carbon dots produced from bio coal can be self-doping with nitrogen on the surface of carbon dots. Generally, the static quenching of BCDs occurs due to the quencher (Fe²⁺), resulting in no fluorescence emission when BCDs and Fe²⁺ are introduced as shown in Fig. 12a. Furthermore, the complex interaction process between the various functional groups of BCDs and heavy metal ions, Fe2+, can result in non-fluorescent complex reactions at the ground state due to the static quenching process (SQE), shown in Fig. 12 -b.

Table 5 represents the fluorescence (FL) lifetime decay of BCDs in the presence and absence of Fe2+ with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and an emission wavelength of 401 nm. The results show that the average FL lifetime decay of BCDs is 8.85 τ/ns, while BCDs with Fe2+ have a lifetime of 8.52 τ/ns, indicating no significant difference in FL lifetime decay between them. This implies that static quenching occurred in the sensor system.

To better understand the relationship between BCDs and Fe2+ metal ions, the UV-Vis of BCDs at different concentrations of Fe2+ was analyzed. The UV-Vis spectra of BCDs with different concentrations of Fe2+ (ranging from 10 µM to 100 µM) showed significant changes in the n-π* region, shown in Fig. 9-d.

It observed that a higher concentration of Fe2+ results in more quenching with BCDs, which in turn causes a greater reduction in the absorption peak at the n-π* region. This indicates that carbon functional groups containing oxygen, specifically the n-π* transition of CO/C-O of BCDs, are highly reactive with Fe2+. As a result, there is more static quenching with a higher concentration of Fe2+. Furthermore, the energy gap of BCDs is 2.81 eV, calculated from the Tauc Plot of Carbon dots (BCDs), shown in Fig. S1.

Tests were conducted on tap and seawater samples to further demonstrate the proposed method's effectiveness. The results of these tests are presented in Table 5, which shows the recovery values of Fe2+ in the samples. The values ranged from 94.86 to 101.6 for tap water and 98.23–105.4 for seawater (Xinghai Bay). These findings suggest that carbon dots synthesized from bio-coal can be used to determine Fe2+ in different water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis of carbon nanodots from bio coal and commercial coal
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots synthesized from bio-coal (B-CDs) exhibit competitive quantum yields (QY) when compared with other carbon-based nanomaterials. The B-CDs in this study demonstrated a QY of approximately 41 %, significantly higher than many traditional coal-based carbon dots (QY ranging from 3 % to 14 %) as shown in Table 6. This superior QY is attributed to the enhanced photostability and the unique surface functionalization achieved through the use of bio-coal as a precursor, which introduces rich sp² and sp³ carbon structures that enhance fluorescence efficiency.
This property makes them particularly useful in environmental monitoring, biomedical imaging, and sensing applications where high signal-to-noise ratios are essential.

In comparison, despite being cost-effective and easier to produce, other coal-derived carbon dots suffer from lower QYs, limiting their practical use in high-sensitivity applications. The lower photoluminescence efficiency of these alternatives often results in weaker signals, making them less suitable for detecting low concentrations of analytes. Furthermore, the bio-coal B-CDs' synthesis process is relatively scalable and cost-effective, enhancing their feasibility for industrial-scale production while maintaining high performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scalability considerations of carbon dots from bio coal
ce:section.ce:para: Coal-based carbon dots (C-CDs) have garnered significant interest due to their unique properties and potential applications in biomedicine, imaging, catalysis, and sensing. Scalability is crucial for the production of carbon dots, and key considerations include precursor selection, synthesis methods, yield and purification systems, toxicity assessment, and cost-effectiveness.

Table 6 compares the scalability, material cost, synthesis cost, and operational cost of carbon dots production using different precursors and synthesis processes, highlighting the quantum yield of carbon dots from bio-coal and commercial coals. Bio-coal as a precursor is vital for scalability due to its renewability, lower environmental impact, local availability, cost-effectiveness, and enhanced functional properties, making it essential for sustainable large-scale production.

Scalable synthesis methods are paramount for B-CD production. Oxidation with hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) has emerged as a promising approach due to its mild conditions, simple composition, and ease of separating B-CDs from the reaction solution. Other methods, such as hydrothermal treatment, microwave synthesis, and electrochemical synthesis, have also been investigated but present limitations for scalability.

Achieving high yields and efficient, easy purification is necessary for scalable B-CD production. Notably, B-CDs can achieve a yield of 41 %, higher than those produced from commercial coal. Optimizing reaction parameters such as temperature, time, and H₂O₂ concentration can further improve yields. Utilizing biomass waste and low-cost bio-coal precursors, along with simple and green synthesis methods and efficient purification techniques, can help reduce production costs. Developing cost-effective and scalable B-CD production processes is essential for large-scale production.

This study highlights the significant potential of bio-coal derived from rice husk pyrolysis oil as both a sustainable fuel source and a precursor for carbon nanodots (BCDs). The thermal polymerization and condensation of pyrolysis bio-oil produce carbon-rich insoluble solid residues with low ash content, a favorable fuel ratio, higher heating values, and combustion characteristic index CCI is higher. These properties make the bio-coal comparable to commercial fossil fuels like coal.

The resulting bio coal is a complex organic mixture with sp² and sp³ carbon hybridizations and various oxygen-containing functional groups (C-O-C, C-O, and CO), making them ideal precursors for synthesizing carbon nanodots. Oxidative cutting with H₂O₂ and purification of bio-coal produce carbon nanodots with a well-crystallized graphite structure, containing nitrogen and oxygen-rich functional groups.

The BCDs exhibit excellent optical properties, including excitation-dependent fluorescence and exceptional thermal and photostability. Notably, the BCDs show selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching towards Fe²⁺ ions, with a low detection limit of 0.33 μM. This quenching mechanism is attributed to the static interaction between Fe²⁺ and the oxygen-containing functional groups on the carbon dots' surface, confirmed by UV-Vis, FTIR, and fluorescence lifetime analyses. The practical applicability of the BCDs was validated by successful Fe²⁺ detection in real water samples, including tap water and seawater, with recovery rates of 94.86–105.4 %.
This innovative approach offers a sustainable solution by utilizing pyrolysis bio-oil as a clean fuel and a precursor for carbon nanomaterials.

Thus, this study represents a significant advancement in exploring new applications of pyrolysis bio-oil in nanotechnology, paving the way for further sustainable and environmentally friendly developments.
